The experiments would be clearer if done with standard LM with Transformers encoder model like BERT for example.
review.explanation: The authors used a better model than BERT whcih enabled extensive experimentation as compared to using BERT

review.segment_text: At the end of Section 2, the authors mention about "generalizations", but I couldn't really find any discussion about this in the paper.
Maybe this can be clarified?

I found that claiming script bias in character models is too strong, if the experiment only shows bias in ZH (and this is expected since its character-level has different notion with languages with Latin script).
Byte-level: I found that there is still some "erraticity" in Figure 3(b) especially when the data size increases (which is more practical in real world application), so this is not entirely resolved?

I also think the summary in Section 1.2 stating that linguistic typological information is not necessary given "statistical properties concerning sequence length and vocabulary" is not necessarily valid since these two properties are the results from linguistic typology information.
Missing references:
From characters to words to in between: Do we capture morphology? Clara Vania and Adam Lopez. ACL 2017.
Multilingual Part-of-Speech Tagging with Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory Models and Auxiliary Loss. Barbara Plank, Anders Søgaard and Yoav Goldberg. ACL 2016.
Review:
The paper provides an empirical investigation of an important problem: the transferability of language modeling signals across languages in the transformer model.
This is an important question because it can teach us both on the relations between languages and the properties of the transformer model (although it is not easy to tease the two effects apart).
This is a thorough paper with a large number of experiments and with interesting conclusions that nicely generalize the low level patterns observed in the experiments.
These conclusions are likely to be useful for the research community as part of its on going investigation of language transfer and the transformer model.
I have several comments though:
The language of the paper is often very complicated.

Just as a couple of examples: It was very hard for me to follow the abstract, the first paragraph, the (very long) sentence that start with "in order to eliminate" (1.1), item 3 in the list of contributions and this is just a partial list.
I ask that if the paper is accepted the authors will try to improve this aspect.
The writing is often over pedagogical and I often got the feeling that the authors try to educate their readers (but not in the positive sense of the word).
I would try to avoid this style.
This work seems highly relevant to the following paper:
Towards Zero-shot Language Modeling. Edoardo Maria Ponti, Ivan Vulic,Ryan Cotterell, Roi Reichart and Anna Korhonen. EMNLP 2019
I think the discussion parts can gain form comparing the conclusions of the two papers, when relevant.

title: Think Big, Teach Small: Do Language Models Distil Occam’s Razor?
paper_text:
Think Big, Teach Small: Do Language Models Distil Occam’s Razor?

 Anonymous Author(s) AfﬁliationAddress email

Abstract Large language models have recently shown a remarkable ability for few-shot 1 learning, including patterns of algorithmic nature. It is now time to ask what kind 2 of patterns these models can capture and how many examples they need in their 3 prompts. We frame this question as a teaching problem with strong priors, and study 4 whether language models can identify simple algorithmic concepts from small 5 witness sets. In particular, we explore how several GPT architectures, program 6 induction systems and humans perform in terms of the complexity of the concept 7 and the number of additional examples, and how much their behaviour differs. 8 This ﬁrst joint analysis of language models and machine teaching can address key 9 questions for artiﬁcial intelligence and machine learning, such as whether some 10 strong priors, and Occam’s razor in particular, can be distilled from data, making 11 learning from a few examples possible.

 12

1 Introduction 13

 An effective and reliable interaction between humans and machines depends on a proper understanding 14 of their priors. The alignment between their priors is especially critical for communication and 15 learning (Sun et al., 2020). For instance, after a human utters the prompt “go stop go", another human 16 would expect “stop" to be a more likely continuation than “go". On the contrary, after the prompt 17 “ready steady" we would expect “go" to be more likely. The former depends on an abstract pattern 18 intrinsic to the sequence (a simple algorithmic sequence alternating ‘go’ and ‘stop’), while the latter 19 depends on some language pattern extrinsic to the sequence (common use in the English language). 20 Language models are the most representative AI systems extrapolating sequences of symbols, and 21 are very good at exploiting the extrinsic patterns that are borrowed from humans after training 22 over massive natural language corpora. However, it is only recently, with systems such as BERT 23 (Devlin et al., 2018), GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019), and GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), that we see 24 how these models can also discover some intrinsic patterns in the prompts provided. This kind of 25 inference is called ‘few-shot learning’, even if there is no further training or ﬁne-tuning of the model. 26 Many of these language models are based on transformers (attention-based architectures, Vaswani 27 et al. (2017)), which do not feature recursion or loops, unlike some approaches based on recurrent 28 neural networks. Some particularly well-chosen and optimised prompts have shown remarkable 29 extrapolations (Xu et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2020; Hendrycks et al., 2021b), illustrating this 30 new kind of abstraction on the go . However, it is unclear how elaborate these abstractions can be and 31 whether they can even have an algorithmic nature. A more systematic analysis is needed to determine 32 the optimal prompts that make the model capture a range of different patterns. 33 Precisely, machine teaching is the area of artiﬁcial intelligence that focuses on determining the 34 optimal examples or curriculum that a teacher should use to make a learner capture a concept (Zhu, 35 2015). This is of enormous importance today in applications such as digital assistants, where we 36 Submitted to 35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021). Do not distribute. would like to teach them new concepts or routines with a few examples, in the same way humans 37 teach or communicate with other humans (Cakmak and Thomaz, 2014; Goodman and Frank, 2016; 38 Degen et al., 2020). It is also fundamental for explainable AI, as the dual problem appears: how a 39 machine can teach (explain) a concept to a human in an efﬁcient way (Khan et al., 2011; Basu and 40 Christensen, 2013). While machine teaching has a long story of theoretical results about what can be 41 taught with a ﬁnite number of examples, it is only recently that the problem has been set in terms of 42 the interactive alignment between the priors of learner and teacher (Goodman and Frank, 2016; Yang 43 and Shafto, 2017; Melo et al., 2018). When dealing with rich representation languages (e.g., regular 44 languages or Turing-complete languages), closer or similar to human language in expressive power, it 45 has recently been shown that strong simplicity priors can make effective teaching possible with very 46 few examples: the overall size of the example set is usually shorter than the algorithmic expression of 47 the concept itself (Telle et al., 2019). For instance, teaching the concept of what an odd number is 48 becomes shorter by using a few examples than by giving the full deﬁnition of what an odd number is. 49 Language models and machine teaching can now meet because of this new few-shot learning ability 50 of language models. We can explore the question of how many examples are needed (or how long they 51 have to be) to teach something to a language model. These are seen as teaching prompts : we want 52 the language model to continue the sequence as if it had captured the pattern that has been expressed 53 in the prompt. From the machine teaching perspective, language models are general systems, as 54 they have not been trained for simplicity or for any particular domain, other than a partial sample of 55 human knowledge expressed as natural language. 56 With their conjunction, we can now ask key questions for AI such as the relevance of simplicity or 57 acquired priors to make learning from a few examples possible (Chater and Vitányi, 2003). In this 58 paper, we examine how a machine teaching setting inspired by Occam’s razor can help us understand 59 how language models ‘learn’ and choose prompts more efﬁciently. We use the term prior ‘distillation’, 60 as opposed to the concept known as knowledge distillation within the ﬁeld of machine learning, as 61 we analyse whether a simplicity prior is acquired as the result of training large language models such 62 as GPT-2 or GPT-3. To complete the picture, we also evaluate some other AI systems (e.g., program 63 induction systems) and humans on the same task, which are known to have this prior. 64 The key contributions of this paper are: 65 • To our knowledge, this is the ﬁrst paper analysing language models in the context of machine 66 teaching. 67 • This is also the ﬁrst paper comparing the acquired priors of language models with humans 68 and program induction systems, when facing patterns that can be taught efﬁciently with a 69 simplicity prior. 70 • We ﬁnd that language models such as GPT-3 have similar or better performance than 71 humans as the number of parameters increase. The performance negatively correlates with 72 the complexity of the concept. 73 • Language models and humans differ in their capability for producing explanations. The 74 length of human explanations correlates with the complexity of the concept. 75 Having a better understanding of language models and its relation to the way other systems, including 76 humans, make inferences is fundamental research that can make the use of these systems safer 77 and more effective, through more robust prompts to solve a variety of tasks. However, this better 78 understanding can also be used maliciously to cunningly modify prompts that alter the results of 79 some applications built on top of language models. A query may fail when using dates, numbers or 80 other strings that have intrinsic patterns. For instance, GPT-3 usually continues “Mary’s father has 5 81 daughters, Lucy, Anne, Alice, Jane and” correctly, but fails on “Mary’s father has 5 daughters, Ba, 82 Baba, Bababa, Babababa and”, systematically continued with ‘Bababababa’. 83 The rest of the paper is organised as follows. In the next section, we present related work in machine 84 teaching, language models and the comparison of machine and human learning. In Section 3, we 85 introduce our theoretical framework, the notion of the teaching size and the importance of the 86 alignment of priors between teacher and learner. Section 4 contains the experimental design, and 87 introduces the agents we consider. Section 5 presents and analyses the results. Finally, we close the 88 paper with a discussion of the implications of this work. 89 2

2 Related Work 90

 It is only recently that deep language models are starting to show some minimal capabilities for few- 91 shot learning. The abstraction abilities of language models have been empirically shown by systems 92 such as GPT-3 that solve “tasks which require to perform simple on-the-ﬂy computational reasoning, 93 recognize a novel pattern that is unlikely to have occurred in training, or adapt quickly to an unusual 94 task” (Brown et al., 2020). Examples of these tasks are simple arithmetic operations and the induction 95 of symbolic manipulations such as anagrams or reversed words. But, are deep language models just 96 memorising a huge corpus, or do they have the capacity of learning abstract concepts from data? This 97 is still an open debate. On the one hand, recurrent neural networks, such as long-short-term-memory 98 (LSTM) architectures, have shown the capacity of capturing syntax-sensitive generalisations such 99 as long distance number agreement (Lakretz et al., 2019), or language modelling for the game of 100 chess (Toshniwal et al., 2021). From a more general perspective, the abstract reasoning capacity of 101 transformers is shown by Hupkes et al. (2020). On the other hand, there are important limitations of 102 language models on simple generalisation tasks (Bender and Koller, 2020; Marcus and Davis, 2020). 103 The comparison between humans and AI systems has usually been performed with systems that are 104 trained for a task, but not for a variety of tasks that are introduced with a few words or examples. 105 For instance, Kühl et al. (2020) present an experiment that compares the performance of several 106 machine learning algorithms and 44 human participants recognising patterns in 3x3 matrices. The 107 results in this case show that humans, as expected, tend to achieve better performance when few 108 examples are provided. This work highlights that many AI systems are still not ready for a direct and 109 fair comparison with humans when limited examples are given for a range of tasks. Further insight 110 can be obtained when we try to look into the extracted models through explanations, and analyse 111 them in the context of concept complexity. For instance, the relation between complexity of concepts 112 and explanations is discussed by Ai et al. (2021). 113 Regarding language models, Hendrycks et al. (2021b) compare the performance of a text model with 114 humans on 57 tasks including elementary mathematics, US history, law, and more. Only GPT-3 115 improves signiﬁcantly over random chance, but the model is still very far from human performance. 116 Poor performance is also shown for code generation when using text descriptions and a few examples 117 (Hendrycks et al., 2021a). Sinha et al. (2019) show that language models obtain poor robustness and 118 generalisation capabilities when compared to graph neural networks that work with symbolic inputs. 119 The experiments also show that humans (with limited time) performed well in simple problems but 120 the performance decreases with complex examples. Motivated by these limitations, McClelland et al. 121 (2019) propose a modiﬁcation of the language models architecture to extend the ability of these 122 systems to consider context information inspired in the way humans use languages. 123 Machine teaching has been previously used to understand how humans teach machines, comparing a 124 machine teaching setting with a curriculum learning setting (Khan et al., 2011; Basu and Christensen, 125 2013). Many of these studies use feature vector concepts instead of rich representations (Turing- 126 complete) such as natural language. Only recently has machine teaching focused on universal 127 languages, and how relevant simplicity priors become when teaching concepts with very few —or 128 very short— examples (Telle et al., 2019). Machine teaching can determine the minimal set of 129 examples (in number or size) that a learner needs to identify a concept, provided teacher and learner 130 share the same prior. In this paper we propose to do this conversely: by generating examples going 131 from these minimal sets to more redundant sets, we can investigate the priors that the learners have. 132

3 Machine Teaching and Priors

 133 Machine teaching is often understood as an inverse problem to machine learning (Zhu, 2015). In 134 machine learning, the aim is to induce a concept from a training set. In machine teaching, given a 135 concept, the goal is to obtain the optimal training set from which a learner is able to learn the concept. 136 Most of the early research in machine teaching was based on the idea of the teaching dimension , 137 the minimum number of examples the learner needs to identify a concept. However, the teaching 138 dimension approach is too narrow. For instance, in the case of teaching concepts from richer 139 languages, it has been demonstrated that even if the teaching dimension is small (low cardinality), 140 the examples might be enormously large. Telle et al. (2019) propose a machine teaching framework 141 that is based on the size of examples instead of the dimension of the witness set. While the teaching 142 3 dimension is related to algorithmic learning theory, the teaching size is more appropriate for language 143 models and constructive examples. For instance, considering two witness sets of the same teaching 144 dimension, the example '(010101,1)' contains more information than '(0,1)'. 145 Following Telle et al. (2019), we consider a possibly inﬁnite example space X and a possibly inﬁnite 146 concept class C consisting of concepts over X . Examples are deﬁned as pairs (cid:104) i, o (cid:105) and concepts 147 as functions mapping inputs to outputs. An example set S = {(cid:104) i 1 , o 1 (cid:105) , . . . , (cid:104) i k , o k (cid:105)} is just a ﬁnite 148 set of input-output pairs, used as witness for the teaching process. Given a concept c ∈ C and an 149 example set S , we say that c satisﬁes S , denoted by c (cid:15) S , if c ( i ) = o for all the pairs (cid:104) i, o (cid:105) in S . The 150 empty set is satisﬁed by all the concepts. We deﬁne an encoding function δ as the number of bits 151 needed to encode S , then δ ( S ) represents the size of S . The teaching size is deﬁned as: 152 S A concept c can be implemented in a language L by zero, one or more programs. A program p 153 captures concept c iff for every S we have that p (cid:15) S iff c (cid:15) S . The equivalence class for c is 154 Class L ( c ) = { p : ∀ S, p (cid:15) S ⇐⇒ c (cid:15) S } . The learner Φ can be seen as a function mapping sets to 155 programs. After this, the teaching size for Φ can be reformulated as follows: 156 Priors can be used as more than one concept might be consistent with one given set S . One natural 157 choice is simplicity, which can be formally deﬁned as the size of the concepts. Let l ( p ) be the length in 158 bits of a program p in L using an appropriate encoding. Let ≺ be the total order of programs ordered 159 by l , where shorter programs precede longer ones. In case of equal l , ties are broken lexicographically. 160 So the teaching size and the learner can now be expressed as: 161 From these equations the concept of the teaching book derives easily. The teaching book is a list 162 consisting of entries in the form (cid:104) w, p (cid:105) being w the optimal witness set and p the smallest program 163 compatible with w . The book is ordered by the encoding size δ for w . The algorithm enumerates 164 on the size of w and looks for the shortest program p in each case, and does not include 〈 w , p 〉 if p 165 has appeared before. All this ensures that no w is repeated and no p is repeated. This setting allows 166 to prove theoretically and empirically that it is possible to ﬁnd witness sets for explaining concepts 167 whose size is smaller than the programs they identify, “an illuminating justiﬁcation of why machine 168 teaching from examples makes sense at all” (Telle et al., 2019). 169 In this work, we build on the increasing relevance of the alignment of priors between learner and 170 teacher (Goodman and Frank, 2016; Yang and Shafto, 2017; Melo et al., 2018) to investigate how 171 long witness sets have to be for different learners (language models, program induction systems and 172 humans), when devising the ‘teaching examples’ using simplicity (Occam’s razor) as a prior. 173

4 Experimental Setting

 174 The questions we want to explore experimentally are: 175 • Q1 - Abstraction capability: Is a language model such as GPT able to capture algorithmic 176 patterns from very few examples selected with a simplicity prior? 177 • Q2 - Learning curves: Does accuracy increase from the theoretically minimal witness sets 178 needed for teaching the concept with additional examples? 179 • Q3 - Characteristic curves: How do language models, program induction approaches and 180 humans behave in terms of the complexity of the patterns? 181 • Q4 - Comparison: How do language models compare with humans and program induction 182 approaches in terms of performance curves? 183 • Q5 - Architecture: Apart from being more capable, which has already been shown before, 184 do larger models have a stronger preference for simple concepts than smaller models? 185 • Q6 - Explanations: Can we get explanations from GPT for these algorithmic patterns? Is the 186 accuracy of human explanations related to performance, and their size related to complexity? 187 Before introducing the experimental setting, we present the algorithmic language we will use and the 188 machine teaching algorithm that selects the optimal examples. 189 4

4.1 The Machine Teaching Setting

 190 The language L we will use to express programs will be the simple but Turing-complete programming 191 language P3, a variation of P'' (Böhm, 1964) with only 7 instructions. We employ P3 as a concept 192 language because it is completely unrelated to the way language models work internally (transformers) 193 and it is different from any natural language and the languages used by the program induction systems. 194 P3 programs operate on an input tape and a write-only output tape, divided into cells, which can 195 hold any of the three characters in the alphabet: Σ = {‘0’, ‘1’, ‘.’}. The pointer is moved using the 196 instructions ‘ < ’ (left) and ‘ > ’ (right). The instructions ‘+’ and ‘ − ’ are used to respectively increase 197 or decrease the value of the pointed cell, in the order they are deﬁned in Σ . The instructions to 198 perform loops are ‘[’ and ‘]’, being ‘[’ the start and ‘]’ the end of the loop, exiting the loop when the 199 pointer is on ‘.’. Finally, the instruction ‘o’ is used to output the value of a pointed cell. 200 We follow Telle et al. (2019) to generate P3 concepts and the associated witness sets as teaching 201 examples. The example set is restricted to binary strings, with ‘.’ used as delimiter, padding cells 202 before and after the string. We use a maximum teaching size of 7 (total number of bits in all the inputs 203 and outputs in the witness set). Removing repeated sets and contradictory examples, the example set 204 space is hence comprised of 17,252 example sets. Note that the teaching procedure and its teaching 205 book assumes a perfect model of the learner (the teacher knows the learner prior). 206

4.2 Choice of Concepts and Examples 207

 We extract eight different concepts c 1 , . . . , c 8 , each c i being randomly selected from all programs in 208 the Teaching Book with i instructions. We bin the concepts into four levels: the programs with 1-2 209 operators (very low complexity, VL-C), those with 3-4 operators (low complexity, L-C), those with 210 5-6 operators (high complexity, H-C) and those with 7-8 operators (very high complexity, VH-C). 211 The minimal witness sets for these concepts in the teaching book are all between 1 and 3 examples. 212 This will be the ﬁrst batch (the witness set, WS) used as training set. As stated before, in this paper 213 we want to test if language models (and other learners, including humans) are capable of capturing 214 patterns when a strong simplicity prior is assumed. As the learner prior might differ from the teacher 215 prior, we want to analyse how learning improves as more examples are provided. Therefore, we will 216 use 5 additional examples in two different batches, a ﬁrst batch with 2 extra examples (additional set 217 one, AS I) and a second batch with 3 extra examples (additional set two, AS II). 218 For the test examples, we generate 5 examples per concept, totalling 8 × 3 × 5 = 120 score values. 219 This number of concepts, batches and test examples aims to ﬁnd a trade-off for a minimally robust 220 evaluation and the limitations of conducting the experiments with humans. 221 With all this, the proposed teaching protocol for each ‘concept lesson’ is divided into three phases. 222 In all cases we ask the learner to complete the test examples. In the ﬁrst phase, we present only the 223 witness set (WS). In the second phase, we also provide the learner the examples of AS I (WS + AS I). 224 Finally, in the third phase, we show all the available examples (WS + AS I + AS II). ,'0'), ('11010','1'), ('0010', '0')} c 4 o+oo If input is '', print '00'; else if input starts with '0', print '001'; else print '1' {('0','011')} {('10','1'),('001','011')} {('00','011'),('0001','011'), ('000','011')} {('01011','011'),('0101','011'),('0010','011'), ('100','1'),('1','1')} 225

4.3 Learners

 226 The following learner families have been considered. 1 227 1 The code and experimental data used in this paper can be found at https://bit.ly/Code3357 5 Humans : We devised a form that was completed by 30 human participants with ages between 18 and 228 54, and 28 of them having university -level education. Apart from the answers to each test example, 229 humans had to write an explanation in natural language for each of the 8 concepts after the third 230 phase. All data collected in the experiments were anonymised, informing the participants of the data 231 treatment and how they could apply their rights, complying with the current data protection laws . 2 232 MagicHaskeller ( MH ): A general-purpose inductive functional programming system based on 233 systematic exhaustive search (Katayama, 2011). It infers programs expressed in Haskell from input- 234 output examples. A strong simplicity prior and a library of Haskell primitives allow MagicHaskeller 235 to solve many problems using only one example. When MH returns more than one hypothesis, we 236 take the ﬁrst one . 237 Louise : An inductive logic programming system based on Top program construction (Patsantzis and 238 Muggleton, 2021). It learns Prolog programs from ﬁnite examples, with the capability of learning 239 recursive programs in polynomial time. Two different approaches are considered, where the left and 240 right recursive calls to the target predicate are omitted (as they end up in inﬁnite loops): 241 • Louise-ens. The most frequent result returned by the totality of the Top program will be 242 used as the ﬁnal answer. Ties are broken randomly. 243 • Louise-exp. The expected score is calculated considering all the possible solutions returned 244 by the Top program. 245 GPT-2 : A transformer-based language model trained on a dataset of 8 million web pages (Radford 246 et al., 2019 ) . The model returns the next predicted word or token considering the previous tokens as 247 the given context. We use the 774M-parameters version of GPT- 2 . The default hyperparameters of 248 the model are preserved, with the exception of the ‘length’ of the output returned, which is modiﬁed 249 to return 3 tokens . By doing so, the experiment execution performance is improved, without asking 250 GPT-2 for more information than what we require for our analysis. Deterministic results can be 251 obtained by setting the ‘top-k’ parameter to 1, but we can get better performance if we use several 252 continuations. Accordingly we will use the default ‘top-k’ value, which is 0. With this, two different 253 approaches will be considered by running the model extrapolations n =40 times for the same query: 254 • GPT-2-ens. The most frequent result (out of the n results obtained for the same query) will 255 be used as the ﬁnal answer. Ties are broken randomly. This approach can be understood as a 256 majority ensemble setting. 257 • GPT-2-exp. Here, the n results for the same query are averaged. This approach can be 258 understood as the expected value for GPT-2 . 259 GPT-3 : The latest approach from the GPT family (Brown et al., 2020). We used different models 260 available, with an increasing number of parameters (Ada, Babbage, Curie and Da Vinci), as recent 261 work (Bender et al., 2021) demonstrate that larger models outperform smaller ones. We want to 262 show whether this trend holds for abstract concepts too, with Occam’s razor emerging more strongly 263 for larger models. We will employ acronyms GPT-3A, GPT-3B, GPT-3C, GPT-3D correspondingly. 264 We performed the experiment considering two different settings: the hyperparameter temperature 265 set to 0 and to 1. When the temperature is set to 1, the results are non-deterministic, and the same 266 approach used in GPT-2-ens is applied. We will use T0 or T1 as shortcuts depending on the value of 267 this parameter. For instance, GPT-3 Da Vinci with temperature 0 will be denoted by GPT-3D-T0. 268 Prompts were designed similarly to GPT-2. Finally, we also asked GPT-3 to provide an explanation 269 of the concepts, just after each batch (8 × 3 explanations). 3 270

5 Results

 271 We take the results of each of the learners in the previous section. For humans, we aggregate the 272 results into a single value per concept, batch and test example, by calculating the average of the 273 30 human subjects. Performance is represented using learning curves and characteristic curves. A 274 learning curve depicts the variation of accuracy according to the number of examples, which in our 275 2 The experiment form used in the human experiments can be found at https://bit.ly/ LearningTest3357 . 3 Complete details of the prompts are included in the supplementary material. 6 Figure presents the average accuracies for seven learning agents shown for the three teaching set batches Interpretation as in Figure 1a Figure b) presents the seven agents shown the four levels Interpretation Figure 1b case is grouped batches WS I II. 276 accuracy C. 277 We start with the analysis for the eight GPT-3 parameter conﬁgurations. The learning curves in Figure 278 1a are clearly increasing in general. The ﬁrst observation is that the systems with the temperature set 279 to 0 obtain better performance than those with the temperature set to 1. There is no clear evidence 280 that larger models in terms of the number of parameters present a better performance for this kind 281 of patterns, which are solely based on simplicity and not other priors. Regarding the characteristic 282 curves in Figure 1b, the GPT-3 systems show a decreasing trend in performance as the complexity 283 increases. Once again, there is no clear pattern in terms of the number of the parameters. Nevertheless, 284 GPT-3D-T0 is the best method, and both GPT-3D versions (the largest models) show their advantage 285 in the very few shot case (WS) and low complexity (VL-C). In other words, more parameters seem to 286 make a difference for easier concepts with fewer examples. 287 Figure 2a shows the learning curves for humans, MH, the two variations for Louise and a selection 288 of GPT methods. GPT-3D-T0 turns out to be the system with the highest average performance for 289 every teaching batch. Louise approaches follow, with a similar performance between them. Humans 290 and GPT-2-ens go next, with similar general performance in the experiment. GPT-2-exp remains 291 slightly under the average performance of the ensemble (majority) approach. Finally, MH is the 292 poorest system, especially for WS, the case with very few examples. For all systems, the accuracy 293 increases as additional examples are provided (AS I and AS II). 294 7 Figure 2b shows the characteristic curves. We see curves cross more often, but overall the order of 295 the learners in terms of performance is similar to the learning curves. The evolution of performance 296 in terms of complexity is generally decreasing, with the best results clearly being for concepts of 297 very low complexity (VL-C). Looking into the results in more detail (see supplementary material). 298 we explain the small increase in the last group (VH-C) as a misalignment between the systems 299 and the teacher, whose representational language is P3. The prior (Occam’s razor) depends on the 300 representational language and the background knowledge in these systems. For instance, if one 301 concept is ‘reverse’ and we have the reverse function in the background knowledge, the program 302 using it is going to be short. As P3 does not have background knowledge, the concept is categorised as 303 complex (it has 8 instructions) even if GPT-3 and Louise, which rely more on background knowledge, 304 can solve it. 305 When looking at the explanations, only those given by MH and humans were minimally acceptable. 306 Despite having tried many kinds of prompts for the GPT systems, as discussed in the supplementary 307 material, we did not get good explanations consistently. For Louise, despite being interpretable, 308 the solutions usually had too many clauses to serve as explanations. As observed in Figure 3a, the 309 percentage of correct natural language explanations decreases as the complexity of the concepts to 310 learn increases. On the other hand, Figure 3b shows how the average size of the correct explanations 311 increases with the complexity of the concepts (size is measured as the number of characters in the 312 natural language explanation given by humans). Remarkably, the average size of the different types 313 of explanations present the opposite behaviour to performance considering the concept complexity 314 bins in Figure 2b. Even for incorrect explanations, the perceived complexity in terms of the size of 315 the explanation inversely corresponds to the accuracy results. 316 Finally, comparing learners by batches of examples or complexity of the concepts as we have seen 317 in the previous plots does not really provide a fully ﬁne-grained metric of how much similar the 318 systems are. This is what we see in Figure 4, showing the error disagreement (proportion of cases 319 where one method is right and the other is wrong, or vice versa) (Figure 4a) and the Phi coefﬁcient 320 (Figure 4b) taking all the examples individually. The matrix for error disagreement strongly depends 321 on performance, so the results for Phi coefﬁcient are more informative. We see that the two Louise 322 approaches have the highest correlation between them. When compared with humans, GPT-3D-T0 323 and Louise-exp are the most similar ones. 324

6 Discussion 325

 We can now answer the questions we formulated in Section 4. We see that only for easy concepts 326 (VL-C), the simplicity prior leads to example sets that humans, Louise and some GPT models can 327 identify consistently (Q1, Q3). For more complex concepts the behaviour is more erratic, and may 328 depend on spurious factors, as the explanations are incorrect for those very hard cases having some 329 accuracy. The learning curves are increasing for all systems, as more examples are given (Q2). 330 8 The minimal set is rarely sufﬁcient, which means that no system prior is perfectly aligned with the 331 simplicity prior that derives from P3 program size. This is expected as the internal representations of 332 these systems are different. Both humans and GPT have priors to capture both extrinsic and intrinsic 333 patterns, as language models have been trained using natural language texts created by humans. This 334 seems consistent with large language models having a comparable performance to humans. However, 335 this would not explain cases in which GPT-3 outperforms humans, unless (1) our population behaves 336 differently (worse) than the population behind the training corpora used for learning them, and/or 337 (2) GPT-3 has generalised some very abstract latent concepts that are applicable for simple intrinsic 338 patterns, where GPT-3 seems to excel (VL-C). Finally, MH is the system that behaves most like 339 an ideal learner in our setting, but with very limited capability. The difference in the performance 340 between MH and Louise (both being inductive programming systems) might be due to the differences 341 in their background knowledge. 342 A more detailed analysis in terms of correlation of errors shows that GPT-3D-T0 is one of the most 343 similar systems to humans (Q4), so the extra performance is not explained by being more deviated 344 from human priors, but perhaps from higher robustness. When we look at architecture size, we do 345 not see a monotonic relation between results and number of parameters (Q5), but the correlation is 346 clear and GPT-3D, for the two variants that we have studied, achieves the best results consistently. 347 These results may suggest that larger models can be better at capturing patterns that depend on 348 simplicity priors, distilling some kind of Occam’s razor for few-shot inference . However, some other 349 experiments in the supplementary material with concepts including loops suggest that larger models 350 in the future may not be able to solve complex algorithmic concepts, with this distillation being 351 limited to sequential (if-then-else) algorithmic patterns. New paradigms beyond transformers may be 352 needed in the future. 353 Explanations should give us more insight about what these systems are doing. Unfortunately, we 354 were not able to ﬁnd a working prompt so that GPT-3 could generate good explanations for the learnt 355 concepts (Q6). Regarding the explanations provided by humans, the results suggest that the higher the 356 complexity, the higher the size of the correct explanations to cover the learnt concepts. Furthermore, 357 as complexity increases, the percentage of correct explanations decreases. 358 There are some limitations in this work. First, while we use MH as a system that would only be 359 dominated by the simplicity priors, it is not very capable, and it is only useful to disentangle the 360 behaviour for very easy concepts. Louise adds robustness to this analysis but it has more dependency 361 on background knowledge. Second, the use of humans for comparisons limits the number of concepts 362 and test examples that we can do for a reasonable questionnaire, and more concepts were tried only 363 for GPT-3 (see supplementary material). Overall, we think that the machine teaching approach can 364 bring very important insights about what language models can do, what their acquired priors are, and 365 how many examples they need. 366 9

review.segment_text: Summary:
This paper attempts to ask whether language models have a bias that allows them to learn from fewer examples in cases where a rule is simpler.
They compare exploitation of rule simplicity in a variety of models, including human participants.

This prior towards simplicity is most strongly observed in larger models, but the minimal example set is rarely enough to train any system, so with the exploitation of simplicity is not a perfect prior.

The paper also provides an analysis of the capability of these systems to provide explanations of the rule, but only humans and the algorithm that they specifically select for this purpose were capable of providing such explanations.
Main Review:

My main complaint with this paper is that it describes what's happening as a simplicity prior, or Occam's razor, but a more in-depth analysis of the pattern they observe might reveal that these systems have a prior towards specific types of rules or methods of compression, such as those that exploit hierarchical patterns or memorized chunks.
review.topic_class_1: complaint about a lack of analysis ot the rule types

review.segment_text: Because there was a lack of analysis of the rule type, focusing only on an abstract idea of simplicity based on a particular method of compression, I found this unconvincing as a study of a general simplicity prior.
review.topic_class_1: complaint about a lack of analysis ot the rule types

review.segment_text: I was most impressed by the fact that there was a human study.
This allows us to compare the priors of an artificial language model with the ability of a human to learn rules.
I would like to see more papers like this, in that respect.
Minor issues / clarity:
I wasn't satisfied with the attempt to explain what a concept was.
review.topic_class_1: concern about the attempt to explain what a concept was

review.segment_text: Within the paper, this is left very vague.
review.topic_class_1: concern about the attempt to explain what a concept was

review.segment_text: What does it mean to say that an explanation is "minimally acceptable"?

A more systematic analysis is needed to determine the optimal prompts that make the model capture a range of different patterns. This sentence seems to indicate that this will be the focus of the paper, but it isn't my understanding of the purpose of the paper.
review.topic_class_2: weakness of experiment weakness of clarity
review.explanation: The reviewer's suggestion is off topic and is out of the scope of this paper.

review.segment_text: most representative AI systems - This is a really unclear claim
I found the example about "go stop go" pretty confusing.
I wonder if you could come up with a better example that doesn't involve iffy phrases like "go stop go stop" or even obscure English idioms like "ready steady go".
I was confused as to the purpose of the paragraph on liens 114-123.
It didn't seem to particularly relate to the rest of the paper.
GPT-3D-T0 is one of the most similar systems to humans - What does this mean?
Grammar:
line 163 "being w"
Limitations And Societal Impact:
Because this paper has such a broad prior it is investigating, the results may be misleading if the true priors are more constrained than "simplicity".
Otherwise, this seems to have a limited impact outside of understanding of the inductive bias of neural networks.

review.segment_text: Summary:
In this paper, the authors compare the few-shot learning abilities of humans and GPT* language models (and inductive programming systems) from a machine teaching point of view.

Models (and humans) are given a minimal witness set (examples using as few bits of information as possible) that should be sufficient to uniquely determine a program (function from a sequence of bits to another) under a pretty strict simplicity prior.
The authors also examine whether additional examples (on top of the minimal witness set) help performance.
They consider 8 different P3 programs of increasing complexity based on the number of operators.
Examples are presented as English prompts.
The minimal witness set is often not sufficient for humans and LMs to generalize correctly.
review.topic_class_1: summary on the experimental conclusions of this paper

review.segment_text: Nevertheless, there is some evidence that GPT models favor simplicity to some degree.
review.topic_class_1: summary on the experimental conclusions of this paper

review.segment_text: Overall, larger GPT models generally perform better than smaller ones, but the correlation is not perfect.
review.topic_class_1: summary on the experimental conclusions of this paper

review.segment_text: GPT-3 models often generalize better than the human candidates based on the few provided examples.
review.topic_class_1: summary on the experimental conclusions of this paper

review.segment_text: However, humans sometimes provide acceptable explanations (for the shorter programs), but the authors could not get GPT models to explain their reasoning.
review.topic_class_1: summary on the experimental conclusions of this paper

review.segment_text: Main Review:
The paper mostly explores LM few-shot learning [1] under the machine teaching setup in [2].
Experiments appear to be designed carefully.
Multiple models are compared, both in terms of program complexity and number of examples given.
The number of programs is low (only 8), which the authors justify as balancing between obtaining robust results and limiting the human effort needed.
I would suggest keeping the current setup for the human comparisons, but greatly expanding the number of programs for the automated systems (much more than in the supplementary material).
Most of the paper is pretty clear, although I had trouble understanding section 3.
review.explanation: The paper is hard to follow and most reviewers agree with that

review.segment_text: In addition to the mathematical definitions, I would appreciate if the authors could explain more thoroughly in plain text.
The findings appear reasonably significant for researchers involved in language modelling and machine teaching.
As language models are commonly used, understanding their behavior, limitations and how they compare to humans seems important.
Other remarks and questions for the authors:
L83: Is it really a failure?
Figure 1 is arguably overcrowded.
It includes changes to both temperature and model size.
For additional clarity, I think these should be presented separately.
In Figure 1b, what is the data condition (WS, WS +AS I, WS + AS I + AS II)?
You group 2 programs under each complexity category.
Do you think that the paper would be more informative if the results of each program were presented on their own, or would it make the paper harder to read?
Does the order of examples matter?
The matrices in Figure 4 are symmetric (along the bottom-left to top-right diagonal), so most information is duplicated.
Why is the size of the additional examples not expressed in bits, as for the minimal witness sets?
Even with additional examples over the minimum witness set, performance of all systems and humans is still far from perfect.
I would be interested in knowing how many examples (or bits) are needed to reach certain performance thresholds (if they are at all reachable).
If I understood correctly, the minimal witness set should allow to uniquely identify a program, under the assumption that shorter programs are preferred.
On line 160, you mention "In case of equal l, ties are broken lexicographically."
Does that mean that if a witness set is compatible with 2 programs of the same length, one program would be chosen arbitrarily?
If that is the case, I would argue that the witness set is inappropriate to explain the chosen concept/program.
Does this phenomenon occur in practice?
[1] Brown et al. (2020). Language models are few-shot learners. NeurIPS.
[2] Telle et al. (2019). The teaching size: computable teachers and learners for universal languages. Machine Learning.
Limitations And Societal Impact:
Mostly yes.
Limitations and societal impacts are discussed, albeit briefly, in the introduction and at the end of the paper.

review.segment_text: Summary:

This paper proposes to empirically investigate to what extent large language models like GPT-3 learn (or "distill") a simplicity prior, i.e., a prior that favors concepts with shorter description length.
The authors investigate this by randomly choosing 8 concepts/programs in the P3 language whose total number of P3 instructions number from 1 to 8, respectively.

Using the framework of Telle et al. (2019), the authors are able to determine the "witness set," the minimal number of examples necessary for a learner with the correct simplicity prior to distinguish the intended concept from others.
This witness set is fed to a learner, which is then evaluated on 5 randomly selected test examples of the concept.
The authors also consider feeding additional sets of 2 and then 3 examples to the learner, each with its own random test set.
The authors evaluate the accuracy of GPT learners, human learners, and 2 inductive program learners on these 3 test sets.
They also elicit explanations from the learners.
Main Review:
This is a very interesting paper, which has undertaken the difficult work of comparing GPT with both human learners and inductive programming learners in a systematic way.

Furthermore, the paper contains some interesting findings, namely, that the advantage of larger GPT models is most pronounced when it comes to smaller, simple concepts, and that GPT models largely outperform humans on the tasks considered.
In terms of presentation, I found the paper difficult to understand at points.

Even after reading section 3 it was unclear to me how witness sets could be so small and what exactly the authors meant by priors being aligned between learner and teacher, and I was only able to understand what the authors intended by reading the Telle et al.
paper.
In particular, I think what I was missing was that witness sets only need to distinguish the shortest satisfying program from earlier, shorter ones.
Since much of section 3 is not used subsequently anyway, I would suggest that the authors instead use the space to emphasize this and how this leads to the experimental setup.
I would also recommend the authors put their GPT prompts and the way they attempted to elicit explanations from GPT in the main text, since it's very hard to interpret any of the results otherwise.
Methodologically, I'm a little concerned that the experiments don't fully address what the authors are after.
In particular, it seems like what we'd really like to know is what concepts the learner has learned, but instead we measure accuracy.
This seems potentially problematic in situations where programs/concepts can have very similar outputs but differ drastically in their complexity, which is presumably the case here.
(For instance, do we have any way of telling from the experiments that GPT hasn't learned the exact desired program except on a list of 10,000 exception inputs where it behaves differently?)
Furthermore, even if we were satisfied with just accuracy, why not get the accuracy on a much larger sample size than 15 per concept (at least for the non-human learners)?
I think if we were able to get reliable explanations from the models we could resolve these questions to some extent, but as the authors note this is difficult to do.
I also wonder whether the machine teaching dimension of the paper changes the story/experiments much.
For instance, if instead of feeding the learners the optimal witness sets the learners just received random examples, would the plots change?
(This experiment might be worth doing!)

If not, these experiments may tell us more about the priors/biases of these learners in general rather than about how these learners perform when being taught, with ramifications for questions Q1 and Q2 in section 4.
Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

review.segment_text: Summary:
This paper studies the type of patterns learned by large pre-trained language models (LMs) in a few-show setting.
The authors differentiate between external patterns (e.g., common-sense or world-knowledge), and internal patterns, such as ABAB, and focus on the latter.

They frame this as a teaching problem with strong priors, and study the size of the example set (number of shots in the few-shot setting) compared to the length of explanation that can be provided to the model in order to teach it the given pattern.
The authors focus on the simplicity prior, and study whether it is implicitly encoded in such large models.
Experiment that compare LMs, program induction systems and humans are performed.
The main findings are that LMs perform as well as humans in few-shot settings, but the two populations differ in their ability to generate explanations.
I would say upfront that I am not an expert in this topic, and have found many points in the paper unclear, perhaps due to this.
However, I wasn't fully convinced by the setup used by the authors.
I am looking forward to the author response that might clarify some of these points.
Main Review:
The main concern I have with this paper is that I am not sure how the concept of witness set as used in this paper is learnable by a machine or a human.

review.segment_text: Summary:
This paper empirically compares humans, language models, and inductive programming systems through the framework of machine teaching.
Concepts and example sets are selected using a simplicity prior with reference to the P3 language.
The main experiments present the example sets to the humans or machines and ask them to infer the corresponding concept.

This setup leads to several experimental questions regarding the alignment between humans and the computational approaches and the extent to which language models like GPT have distilled a simplicity prior.
Main Review:
Note: I am not an NLP researcher so I cannot comment on this work’s significance to that community.
Positive comments:

The basic premise of this paper, to study the intersection of machine teaching and language models, is very interesting, and as far as I know, novel, and I expect there will be follow up work that continues this line of inquiry.
I would therefore give this paper high marks in terms of originality and significance.

The paper is extremely well written at both a sentence level and a high level. I found the whole paper very enjoyable to read and would give high marks in terms of clarity. The experiments are clear and thorough and the supplemental results are extensive.
review.explanation: The paper is hard to follow and most reviewers agree with that

review.segment_text: The overall contribution of the paper is large, clearly requiring an extensive effort, making it well suited for NeurIPS in terms of scope.
review.explanation: This paper focuses on a toy setting and may not fit NeurIPS

review.segment_text: I commend the authors for reporting the experimental question Q6 even though they were not able to answer it affirmatively.
(As an aside, the teacher-student conversation in supplementary materials 8, while a “failure”, is remarkably coherent.)
Concerns and questions:
My biggest concern is that nearly all of the results in the paper are based on the same 8 concepts.
review.topic_class_1: concern about limited number of concepts used

review.segment_text: It is very possible that some of the empirical findings are specific to quirks in these 8 concepts.
review.topic_class_1: concern about limited number of concepts used

review.segment_text: It makes sense that the human experiments needed a limited set, but it would greatly strengthen the paper if additional machine-only results were added with results averaged over hundreds or thousands of concepts.
review.topic_class_1: concern about limited number of concepts used

review.segment_text: I am also concerned about the relative mismatch between the average accuracies of humans (e.g.
Figure 2b) versus their ability to provide correct explanations (Figure 3a).
Should we conclude that humans are sometimes guessing correctly?
Or that there are some quirks in the concepts, where the examples are suggestive of some other concept that is wrong, but happens to align with the test examples?
Also, the relatively low rate of explanation correctness across the board suggests that this task may just be too difficult for humans (it is certainly difficult for me, looking at the examples).
For the “accuracy” results, if I were to guess 2 out of 5 tests correctly for a single concept, would that contribute 0.4 or 0.0 to the accuracy?
Please clarify in the text.
There are a lot of interesting results and points raised in this paper, which is mostly a strength, but there are parts where the narrative feels unfocused and the main takeaways feel unclear.
review.explanation: The reviewer is making vague arguments without providing concrete examples.

review.segment_text: I am wondering if the inclusion of the inductive programming methods (Louise and MH) are more distracting than informative, at least in the way that they are presented now, as first-class citizens alongside the GPT and human results.
review.topic_class_1: question about the inductive programming methods

review.segment_text: My understanding is that the behavior of these methods is much less mysterious than that of GPT or humans -- if the background knowledge given to the inductive programming methods were perfectly aligned with P3, then we would see perfect agreement in the results.
review.topic_class_1: question about the inductive programming methods

review.segment_text: So really the results for these methods are just a function of the background knowledge.
review.topic_class_1: question about the inductive programming methods

review.segment_text: I’m not sure what we are meant to take away from these results in particular.
review.topic_class_1: question about the inductive programming methods

For both humans and GPT, I would be curious to see if the results are robust to changes in the syntax.
review.topic_class_1: question about the robustness of humans ang GPT

review.segment_text: For example, instead of 0 and 1, what about a and b, or apple and banana, # and %, etc.
review.topic_class_1: question about the robustness of humans ang GPT

review.segment_text: When presenting the non-human approaches with WS, then WS + AS I, then WS + AS I + AS II, are the methods “reset” after each phase?
For example, with GPT-3, does it see the full history of phases, or is just given each phase independently?
I am guessing the latter.
If so, is it possible that the humans have an inherent advantage over the machines?
Intuitively, from the sequence, the humans could infer the relative complexity of the examples in each set, giving some additional hints about the target concept.
Limitations And Societal Impact:
See “concerns” above.
